paper.title: Do ethnic disparities exist in disease burden and healthcare utilization of male breast cancer? A 9-year cohort study of 9.08 million adults in China

Do ethnic disparities exist in disease burden and healthcare utilization of male breast cancer? A 9-year cohort study of 9.08 million adults in China

evidence_entities.text: Among 630 participants (mean [SD] age, 59.4 [13.1] years), age-standardized rates of incidence were 1.2 (95% CI: 0.4–2.1) per 100, 000.
Regarding ethnicity, Mongolian had a higher age-standardized 5-year prevalence rate than Han (3.2[95% CI: 2.5–4.0] vs 2.3[95% CI: 1.7–3.0], P=0.016)
no significant differences existed in incidence rates, survival rates, and risk of all-cause and breast cancer-specific mortality
few significant disparities in healthcare utilization between Han and Mongolian were observed
there was a significantly higher proportion of Mongolian patients who used chemotherapy (38.7% vs 25.0%, P=0.020)
BRCA2 mutations were more common in MBC. The BRCA2 mutation was more frequently observed in China than in other Asian countries, resulting in a higher incidence in China.

notes: This study examined ethnic disparities in male breast cancer between Han and Mongolian populations in Inner Mongolia, China
The study found minimal ethnic disparities in disease burden and healthcare utilization
Economic factors were identified as potentially important mediators of ethnic disparities
BRCA2 mutations were noted to be more common in Chinese populations compared to other Asian countries
